name.text: Week 13 – Q5: Bounce Diagram Transient Analysis

questiontext.text:
Bounce Diagram — Transient Analysis

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 10 points (9 parts: a–e worth 1 pt each, f worth 2 pts, g–i worth 1 pt each) | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A lossless transmission line has characteristic impedance \(Z_0 = {@Z0@}\;\Omega\), length \(\ell = {@l_m@}\;\text{m}\), and phase velocity \(v_p = {@vp_disp@} \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}\).

A step-function voltage source \(V_g = {@Vg@}\;\text{V}\) with series resistance \(R_g = {@Rg@}\;\Omega\) drives the line. The load is a resistor \(R_L = {@RL@}\;\Omega\). At \(t = 0\) the switch closes.

Part (a) — Load reflection coefficient (1 pt)

Compute the load reflection coefficient:

\[\Gamma_L = \frac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0}\]

\(\Gamma_L = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.333 or -0.5

Part (b) — Source reflection coefficient (1 pt)

Compute the source (generator) reflection coefficient:

\[\Gamma_g = \frac{R_g - Z_0}{R_g + Z_0}\]

\(\Gamma_g = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.333 or -0.5

Part (c) — Initial forward voltage wave (1 pt)

Compute the amplitude of the initial forward-travelling voltage wave:

\[V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot \frac{Z_0}{R_g + Z_0}\]

\(V_1^{+} = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] V

Syntax hint: Enter a number in Volts, e.g. 6.667

Part (d) — One-way travel time (1 pt)

Compute the one-way travel time of the line:

\[T = \frac{\ell}{v_p}\]

\(T = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] s

Syntax hint: Enter in seconds using scientific notation, e.g. 5e-9

Part (e) — Steady-state voltage at the load (1 pt)

Compute the DC steady-state voltage across the load (after all reflections have died out):

\[V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_g + R_L}\]

\(V_{\text{ss}} = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]] V

Syntax hint: Enter a number in Volts, e.g. 8.0

Part (f) — Voltage waveform at the load (2 pts)

Using the bounce diagram, determine the voltage \(V(z = \ell,\, t)\) at the load as a function of time. Place exactly 4 points on the interactive graph below at times \(t = T,\; 3T,\; 5T,\; 7T\), each at the correct voltage level for that time interval.

[[jsxgraph input-ref-ans6="ans6Ref" width="680px" height="440px"]] var yMax = {#y_max#}; var yMin = {#y_min#}; var Vss = {#ta5#}; var MAX_POINTS = 4; var SNAP = 0.5; var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(divid, { boundingbox: [-0.5, yMax, 8.5, yMin], axis: true, grid: true, showNavigation: true, showCopyright: false }); /* Axis labels */ board.create('text', [8, yMin + (yMax - yMin) * 0.06, 't / T'], { anchorX: 'right', fontSize: 14, fixed: true }); board.create('text', [-0.35, yMax - 2, 'V (V)'], { anchorX: 'right', fontSize: 14, fixed: true }); /* Vss reference line */ board.create('line', [[0, Vss], [1, Vss]], { strokeColor: '#999', strokeWidth: 1.5, dash: 3, straightFirst: true, straightLast: true, fixed: true, highlight: false }); board.create('text', [8.2, Vss, 'V_{ss}'], { anchorX: 'left', fontSize: 12, fixed: true }); /* Vertical guides at t/T = 1, 3, 5, 7 */ var guides = [1, 3, 5, 7]; for (var gi = 0; gi < guides.length; gi++) { board.create('line', [[guides[gi], 0], [guides[gi], 1]], { strokeColor: '#ccc', strokeWidth: 1, dash: 2, straightFirst: true, straightLast: true, fixed: true, highlight: false }); } /* Point management */ var pts = []; var labels = []; var stateInput = document.getElementById(ans6Ref); /* Build the "Your placed points" table dynamically inside the JSXGraph div so that DOM lookups always succeed (static HTML outside [[jsxgraph]] is not guaranteed to be in the same DOM scope in all STACK versions). */ var tableHTML = '
' + '

Your placed points

' + '' + '' + '' + '' + '' + '' + '
Pointt / TVoltage
P1\u2014\u2014
P2\u2014\u2014
P3\u2014\u2014
P4\u2014\u2014
'; var boardDiv = document.getElementById(divid); var tableWrapper = document.createElement('div'); tableWrapper.innerHTML = tableHTML; boardDiv.parentNode.insertBefore(tableWrapper, boardDiv.nextSibling); /* Cache direct references to table cells (avoids repeated getElementById) */ var cellRefs = []; for (var ci = 0; ci < MAX_POINTS; ci++) { cellRefs.push({ x: tableWrapper.querySelector('#q5-p' + (ci+1) + '-x'), y: tableWrapper.querySelector('#q5-p' + (ci+1) + '-y') }); } function snapVal(v) { return Math.round(v / SNAP) * SNAP; } function serializePoints() { if (pts.length === 0) { stateInput.value = '[]'; } else { var parts = []; var sorted = pts.slice().sort(function(a, b) { return a.X() - b.X(); }); for (var i = 0; i < sorted.length; i++) { parts.push('[' + snapVal(sorted[i].X()) + ',' + snapVal(sorted[i].Y()) + ']'); } stateInput.value = '[' + parts.join(',') + ']'; } /* Dispatch change event so STACK picks up the new value */ if (stateInput) { stateInput.dispatchEvent(new Event('change')); } } function addPoint(x, y) { if (pts.length >= MAX_POINTS) return null; var snx = snapVal(x); var sny = snapVal(y); var p = board.create('point', [snx, sny], { name: '', size: 5, strokeColor: '#0055d4', fillColor: '#3388ff', snapToGrid: true }); var idx = pts.length; var lbl = board.create('text', [ function() { return snapVal(p.X()) + 0.25; }, function() { return snapVal(p.Y()) + (yMax - yMin) * 0.04; }, function() { return 'P' + (idx+1) + ' (' + snapVal(p.X()) + ', ' + snapVal(p.Y()) + ')'; } ], { fontSize: 11, color: '#0055d4', fixed: true, highlight: false }); p.on('drag', function() { serializePoints(); updateTable(); }); pts.push(p); labels.push(lbl); updateTable(); return p; } function getMouseCoords(e) { var cPos = board.getCoordsTopLeftCorner(e, 0), absPos = JXG.getPosition(e, 0), dx = absPos[0] - cPos[0], dy = absPos[1] - cPos[1]; return new JXG.Coords(JXG.COORDS_BY_SCREEN, [dx, dy], board); } /* Restore state */ if (stateInput && stateInput.value && stateInput.value !== '[]') { var matches = stateInput.value.match(/\[(-?[\d.]+),\s*(-?[\d.]+)\]/g); if (matches) { for (var i = 0; i < matches.length && i < MAX_POINTS; i++) { var nums = matches[i].match(/(-?[\d.]+)/g); if (nums && nums.length >= 2) { addPoint(parseFloat(nums[0]), parseFloat(nums[1])); } } serializePoints(); updateTable(); } } /* Click handler */ board.on('down', function(e) { if (e[JXG.touchProperty]) return; var coords = getMouseCoords(e); var dominated = false; for (var el in board.objects) { if (JXG.isPoint(board.objects[el]) && board.objects[el].hasPoint(coords.scrCoords[1], coords.scrCoords[2])) { dominated = true; break; } } if (dominated || pts.length >= MAX_POINTS) return; var x = snapVal(coords.usrCoords[1]); var y = snapVal(coords.usrCoords[2]); if (x < 0 || x > 8 || y < yMin || y > yMax) return; addPoint(x, y); serializePoints(); }); function updateTable() { for (var i = 0; i < MAX_POINTS; i++) { var xCell = cellRefs[i].x; var yCell = cellRefs[i].y; if (xCell && yCell) { if (i < pts.length) { xCell.textContent = snapVal(pts[i].X()).toString(); yCell.textContent = snapVal(pts[i].Y()).toString() + ' V'; } else { xCell.innerHTML = '\u2014'; yCell.innerHTML = '\u2014'; } } } } /* Reset button */ board.create('button', [5.5, yMax - 1.5, 'Reset', function() { for (var i = pts.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { board.removeObject(labels[i]); board.removeObject(pts[i]); } pts = []; labels = []; board.update(); serializePoints(); updateTable(); }]); [[/jsxgraph]]

Click on the graph to place 4 key points showing the voltage \(V(t)\) at the load. Place points at times \(t = T,\; 3T,\; 5T,\; 7T\) with the correct voltage level at each step. You can drag points to adjust. The dashed grey line shows the steady-state voltage \(V_{\text{ss}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\).

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Part (g) — Settling analysis (1 pt)

Look at the voltage values you placed in Part (f). Notice how each successive voltage step gets smaller as the reflections decay — this is because each round trip multiplies the wave amplitude by \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g\).

After how many wave arrivals at the load does the voltage \(V(z = \ell)\) settle to within 5% of the steady-state value \(V_{\text{ss}}\)?

In other words, find the smallest integer \(n\) such that \(|V_n - V_{\text{ss}}| < 0.05 \cdot |V_{\text{ss}}|\), where \(V_n\) is the voltage at the load after the \(n\)-th wave arrival (at time \(t = (2n-1)T\)).

\(n = \) [[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Syntax hint: Enter an integer, e.g. 3

Part (h) — Comparing settling behaviour (1 pt)

In this circuit, \(\Gamma_L = {@ta1@}\) and \(\Gamma_g = {@ta2@}\), so the round-trip reflection product is \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g = {@settle_product_disp@}\).

A colleague designs a different transmission line system where \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g = 0.9\) (both source and load are heavily mismatched). Compared to your system, how would the settling behaviour differ?

[[input:ans8]] [[validation:ans8]]

Select the most physically accurate comparison.

Part (i) — Design constraint for fast settling (1 pt)

If you wanted the voltage at the load to settle within 5% of \(V_{\text{ss}}\) after exactly 2 wave arrivals (\(n = 2\)), what constraint does this place on the product \(|\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g|\)? Show your reasoning.

[[input:ans9]] [[validation:ans9]]

Write a brief derivation. This part is graded for completion; a model answer is provided after submission.

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Load Reflection Coefficient \(\Gamma_L\)

The load reflection coefficient is:

\[\Gamma_L = \frac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0} = \frac{{@RL@} - {@Z0@}}{{@RL@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta1@}\]

Step 2: Generator Reflection Coefficient \(\Gamma_g\)

The generator (source) reflection coefficient is:

\[\Gamma_g = \frac{R_g - Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = \frac{{@Rg@} - {@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta2@}\]

Step 3: Initial Forward Voltage Wave \(V_1^{+}\)

At \(t = 0\), the source drives the line through a voltage divider formed by \(R_g\) and \(Z_0\):

\[V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot \frac{Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = {@Vg@} \cdot \frac{{@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\]

Step 4: One-Way Travel Time \(T\)

The one-way travel time is simply the line length divided by the phase velocity:

\[T = \frac{\ell}{v_p} = \frac{{@l_m@}}{{@vp@}} = {@ta4@}\;\text{s} \approx {@T_ns@}\;\text{ns}\]

Step 5: Steady-State Voltage \(V_{\text{ss}}\)

After all reflections have died out, the transmission line behaves as a simple wire. The DC steady-state voltage across the load is a resistive divider:

\[V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_g + R_L} = {@Vg@} \cdot \frac{{@RL@}}{{@Rg@} + {@RL@}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\]

Step 6: Bounce Diagram — Voltage at the Load \(V(z = \ell,\, t)\)

At the load (\(z = \ell\)), the voltage changes at odd multiples of the one-way travel time: \(t = T, 3T, 5T, 7T, \ldots\)

The initial forward wave \(V_1^{+}\) arrives at the load at \(t = T\). At each subsequent bounce, the reflected and re-reflected waves accumulate. The voltage at the load after each bounce is:

  • At \(t = T\): The first wave arrives. \[V(T) = V_1^{+}(1 + \Gamma_L) = {@ta3@} \times (1 + {@ta1@}) = {@V_step1@}\;\text{V}\]
  • At \(t = 3T\): After one round trip (reflection at source, then back to load). \[V(3T) = V(T) + V_1^{+} \cdot \Gamma_L \Gamma_g (1 + \Gamma_L) = {@V_step2@}\;\text{V}\]
  • At \(t = 5T\): Second round-trip reflection. \[V(5T) = V(3T) + V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^2 (1 + \Gamma_L) = {@V_step3@}\;\text{V}\]
  • At \(t = 7T\): Third round-trip reflection. \[V(7T) = V(5T) + V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^3 (1 + \Gamma_L) = {@V_step4@}\;\text{V}\]

As the bounces continue, the voltage converges to \(V_{\text{ss}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\).

Step 7: Settling Analysis

Each successive voltage increment at the load is multiplied by \(\Gamma_L \Gamma_g = {@settle_product@}\). The voltage after the \(n\)-th wave arrival at the load is a partial sum of a geometric series. We look for the first \(n\) where \(|V_n - V_{\text{ss}}| < 0.05 \cdot |V_{\text{ss}}|\).

Since \(|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g| = {@abs(settle_product)@} < 1\), the increments decay geometrically. The voltage settles within 5% of \(V_{\text{ss}}\) after \(n = {@ta7@}\) wave arrivals at the load.

Step 8: Comparing Settling Behaviour

The product \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g = {@settle_product_disp@}\) controls how quickly the bouncing voltage converges. A product close to 0 means rapid convergence (each bounce is much smaller than the previous). A product close to 1 (or −1) means slow convergence (each bounce retains most of its amplitude).

With \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g = 0.9\), each round-trip reflection retains 90% of the wave amplitude. This is much slower settling than your system (where the product is {@settle_product_disp@}). The voltage would oscillate many more times before converging to \(V_{\text{ss}}\).

Note: \(|\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g| < 1\) is always true for passive loads, so the system will eventually settle — it is not unstable.

Step 9: Design Constraint for Fast Settling

After the \(n\)-th wave arrival at the load, the remaining error is proportional to \(|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g|^n\). For settling within 5% after \(n = 2\) arrivals:

\[|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g|^2 < 0.05 \implies |\Gamma_L \Gamma_g| < \sqrt{0.05} \approx 0.224\]

This means both the source and load must be reasonably well-matched to the line — for example, \(|\Gamma_L| \approx 0.47\) and \(|\Gamma_g| \approx 0.47\), or one very well matched and the other less so.

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)\(\Gamma_L\){@ta1@}
(b)\(\Gamma_g\){@ta2@}
(c)\(V_1^{+}\){@ta3@} V
(d)\(T\){@ta4@} s
(e)\(V_{\text{ss}}\){@ta5@} V
(f)V(t) at loadPoints at (T, {@V_step1@}), (3T, {@V_step2@}), (5T, {@V_step3@}), (7T, {@V_step4@})
(g)Settling bounces{@ta7@} wave arrivals
(h)Settling comparisonMuch slower settling (retains 90% per bounce)
(i)Design constraint\(|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g| < \sqrt{0.05} \approx 0.224\)

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Γ_L

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Γ_g

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — V₁⁺

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Travel Time T

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — Steady-State V_ss

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (f) — V(t) at Load (JSXGraph)

[[feedback:prt6]]

Part (g) — Settling bounces

[[feedback:prt7]]

Part (h) — Settling comparison

[[feedback:prt8]]

Part (i) — Design constraint

[[feedback:prt9]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: Z0={@Z0@}Ω, Rg={@Rg@}Ω, RL={@RL@}Ω, Vg={@Vg@}V | Γ_L={@ta1@}, V_ss={@ta5@} | n_settle={@ta7@} | settle_product={@settle_product_disp@}

questiondescription.text: Bounce diagram transient analysis — compute reflection coefficients, initial forward wave, travel time, steady-state voltage, and plot the voltage waveform at the load using an interactive JSXGraph. Week 13 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

input.name: ans9
input.syntaxhint: Think about what |Gamma_L * Gamma_g|^n < 0.05 implies when n = 2.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The load reflection coefficient is \(\Gamma_L = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0} = \dfrac{{@RL@} - {@Z0@}}{{@RL@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta1@}\).
Incorrect. The generator reflection coefficient is \(\Gamma_g = \dfrac{R_g - Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = \dfrac{{@Rg@} - {@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta2@}\).
Incorrect. The initial forward wave is found from the voltage divider at the source: \(V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot \dfrac{Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = {@Vg@} \cdot \dfrac{{@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\).

Incorrect. In the DC steady state, the transmission line acts as a simple wire. The voltage across the load is a resistive divider: \(V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_g + R_L} = {@Vg@} \cdot \dfrac{{@RL@}}{{@Rg@} + {@RL@}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\).

node.description: AlgEquiv check — need at least 3 of 4 points correct

truefeedback.text:
Well done! Your V(t) plot is correct.

{@feedback_msg@}

falsefeedback.text:
Not quite right. Check the voltage levels at each bounce.

{@feedback_msg@}

truefeedback.text: Correct! The voltage at the load settles to within 5% of \(V_{\text{ss}}\) after \(n = {@ta7@}\) wave arrivals.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. After each wave arrival at the load, the voltage step decreases by a factor of \(|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g| = {@abs(settle_product)@}\). Compute the cumulative voltage after each arrival and find the first \(n\) where \(|V - V_{\text{ss}}| < 0.05 \cdot |V_{\text{ss}}|\). The correct answer is \(n = {@ta7@}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! With \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g = 0.9\), each round-trip retains 90% of the wave amplitude. This gives much slower convergence than your system where \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g = {@settle_product_disp@}\). The system is not unstable — it converges, but very slowly.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The product \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g\) controls the convergence rate: each successive bounce multiplies the voltage increment by this factor. A value of 0.9 means 90% of the wave amplitude survives each round trip — much more than your system (where the product is {@settle_product_disp@}). The system does converge (it is not unstable), but much more slowly.

truefeedback.text: Thank you for your derivation. Compare your reasoning with the model answer in the worked solution above.

falsefeedback.text: Thank you for your derivation. Compare your reasoning with the model answer in the worked solution above.

hint.text: Hint 1 (Concept): When a step-function voltage source drives a transmission line through a series resistance, the initial voltage wave is determined by the voltage divider between \(R_g\) and \(Z_0\). Each time the wave reaches a discontinuity (load or source), it partially reflects. The reflection coefficients \(\Gamma_L\) and \(\Gamma_g\) control the amplitude of each reflected wave. Over time, the superposition of all waves converges to the DC steady-state value.

Hint 2 (Formulas): The key formulas are:

  • \(\Gamma_L = (R_L - Z_0)/(R_L + Z_0)\) — can be positive or negative
  • \(\Gamma_g = (R_g - Z_0)/(R_g + Z_0)\)
  • \(V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot Z_0 / (R_g + Z_0)\) — voltage divider at source
  • \(T = \ell / v_p\) — one-way travel time
  • \(V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot R_L / (R_g + R_L)\) — DC voltage divider (line is transparent)

Hint 3 (Bounce diagram steps): At the load (\(z = \ell\)), voltage changes at \(t = T, 3T, 5T, 7T, \ldots\):

  1. At \(t = T\): \(V = V_1^{+}(1 + \Gamma_L)\)
  2. At \(t = 3T\): Add the contribution \(V_1^{+} \cdot \Gamma_L \Gamma_g (1 + \Gamma_L)\)
  3. At \(t = 5T\): Add \(V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^2 (1 + \Gamma_L)\)
  4. At \(t = 7T\): Add \(V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^3 (1 + \Gamma_L)\)

Each successive bounce adds a factor of \(\Gamma_L \Gamma_g\) (which has magnitude < 1), so the steps get progressively smaller and converge to \(V_{\text{ss}}\).

For part (g), note that each round-trip multiplies the voltage increment by \(\Gamma_L \Gamma_g\). Compute the cumulative voltage at the load after each wave arrival and check when it first comes within 5% of \(V_{\text{ss}}\).

For part (h), think about what \(\Gamma_L \cdot \Gamma_g\) controls: it determines how much of each bounce survives. A value of 0.9 means 90% survives each round trip. Compare this to your system's value.

For part (i), the error after \(n\) arrivals is proportional to \(|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g|^n\). Set this less than 0.05 with \(n = 2\) and solve for \(|\Gamma_L \Gamma_g|\).
